Fern Por-rrrcer- PRACTTcEs C ounrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramenro, CA g5gl1_2329
(9t6) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
May 6, 2008
Brian Oneto
Amador County Supervisor, 5th District
Office of the Board of Supervisors
810 Court Street
Jackson, CA 95642
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-08-044a
Dear Supervisor Oneto:
This letter further responds to yoLlr request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because your qLlestion seeks general
guidance and is not linrited to-a specific governmental decisior, *. u." treating your request as
one for informal assistance.i Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct
that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to
prospective actions. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fuii potiti"ot
Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FppC Ops. 71.)
Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, oi other conflict-of-interest laws such as
common law conflict of interest or Government code Section 1090.
QUESTION
In light of your multiple property interests within the vicinity of a casino project,
including an interest in a mining claim abutting the site of the projeit and a home/ranch
' The Political Refirrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections g l()00 through 91014. All statutory
referencesaretotheGovernmentCode.unlessotherwiseindicated. TheregulationsoftheFiirRllitical practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l I0 through 18997 of Tirle 2 of thi california Code of Regulations. AII
regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the Califbrnia Corie of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.
r Infbrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion 9r formal
written advice. (Section tt3 | l4; Regularion Ig329(c)( j). copy encl.sed.)


File No. I-08-044a
Page No. 2
approximately one-half mile from the site, do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit
you from making, participating in making, or influencing govemmental deCisions related to
efforts by the Ione Band of Miwoks to take land into t*it ?or the constmction of the proposed
casino?
CONCLUSION
You may not make, participate in making, or influence governmental decisions related to
efforts by the Ione Band of Miwoks to take land into trust for th1 construcrion of the proposed
casino. Specific circumstances exist that make it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decisions will have a material financial effect on your economic interest in your home/ranch.
Additionally, the facts provided indicate a reasonably foreseeable financial Lffect on your
economic interest in a mining claim abutting the site of the project that is presumed n,ur..iul.
FACTS
You are the 5th District Supervisor in Amador County. The Amado. Co*ty Board of
Supervisors (the "Board") will soon be faced with several issues relating to efforts 
-by 
the 1one
Band of Miwoks to obtain Federal approval to take land into trust for a f,roposed gaming casino
in the City of Plymouth. While the Board has previously decided to puisue a state and federal
suit in opposition to the tribe's proposal, this decision *ir made prioi to your term of office. In
a letter to the Commission dated April 7, 2008, Amador County tounsel ias provided that no
Board actions have been taken regarding the tribe's proposal since your term tf office
commenced in January 2007.
You own several property interests within the vicinity of the proposed casino. These
interests include (1) your home/ranch consisting of 222 acrei and tocatei approximately one-half
mile from the casino site, (2) mineral rights on land in the City of Plymouth iocated more than a
one-halfmilefromthecasinosite,(3) agrazingleasecostingbrt:peryearonBureauof Land
Management lands abutting the casino site, and (4) a mining claim vatula at $5,500 on the same
Bureau of Land Management lands abutting the casino site.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the lovernmental
decision will have a material financial effect on one or -t." of the public officlal,s economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step
standard analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a
given governmental decision, which we apply to your quesrion. (Regulation 1g700(bxl)-(g).)


File No. I-08-044a
Page No. 3
Steps One and Two: Are you a "public official" making, participating in making, or
influencing a governmental decision?
You are a "public official" making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision when acting in your role as a county supervisor.3
Step Three: What are your..economic interests?r,
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Secrion 87103(a); Regulation lg703.l(a)); or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703. l(b));
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regularion lg703.Z):
o An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, aggregating $500
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation iAZbf .:i;
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390 or
more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(e); R"grtution 18703.4);
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate
family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (Section gZtO:; Regulation 1g703.5).
From all indications, you have an interest in your home/ranch located approximately one-
half mile from the proposed casino of $2,000 or more. You have also provided^ that the value of
I A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or
her position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to a course of aition, or enters into a
contractual aSreement on behalf of his or her agency. (section 87100; Regulation 18702.1.) A public offlcial
"participates in making a governmental decision" when, acting within the iuthority of his or heiposition and
without significant substantive review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the decision
maker regarding the governmental decision. (Section 87 l00l Regulation 18702.2.) A public oftlcial is attempting to
use his or her otflcial position to influence a decision bef'ore his tir her own agency ii fbr the purpose of intluencing
the decision. the official contacts or appears befbre a member, otflcer, employee, br consultant oihi, o, her agencyl
(Section 87 100; Regulation 18102.3.)


File No. I-08-044a
Page No.4
you mining claima abutting the proposed casino is $5,500. Accordingly you have economic
interests in both of these interests in real property.
we note that your account of the facts also indicates other possible economic interests
including economic interests in any business entity related to your ranching or mining
operations, economic interests in any sources of income related to your ran-ching and mining
operations, and your economic interest in your personal finances. you also have an economic
interest in the mineral rights located in the City of Plymouth if you have an investment of more
than $2000 in these rights. Prior to making, participating in making, or influencing any
governmental decision, it is necessary to consider all economic interests. Howevei, in light of
our conclusion that your economic interests in your home/ranch and the mining claim aU-utting
the proposed casino disqualify you from participating in decisions related to th; proposed casino,
it is unnecessary for us to consider your other eiono*i. irt"r"sts. Accordingly, this advice is
limited to an analysis of your economic interests in your home/ranch and the mining claim.
Step Four: Is your economic interest in real property directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?
Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official has an economic
interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if any of the following appty:
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the govemmental decision. ...
"(2)The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning, annexation
or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion fiom any city,
county, district or other local govemmental subdivision, of the real property in which
the official has an interest or a similar decision affecting the real prop"rty....
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation of
a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use or uses of
the real property in which the official has an interest.
"(4) The govemmental decision involves the imposition, repeal, or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real pirp"rty in which
the official has an interest.
"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select the
project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project ur"u "o.-ittee, to
certify the environmental document, to adopt the redeveiopment plan, to add
I Section 82033 defines "interest in real property" to include "any leasehold, beneficial or ownership
interest or an option to acquire such an interest in ieai prnperty located in ihe jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly
or beneficially by a public otflcial, or other fller, or his or-herlmmediate family if the fair market value of the
interest is two thousand dollars ($2'000) or more." Under this defrnition, your mining claim is an economic interest
in real property as your claim is a beneficial or ownership interest in real property.


File No. I-08-044a
Page No.5
territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above
decisions; and real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of it is
located within the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redevetopment
area.
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to, streets,
water, sewer' storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the
official has an interest will receive new or improved services.,,
Home/Ranch
Because your home/ranch is not located within 500 feet of the property and you have not
provided any facts that the governmental decision involves any of the iypes oi decisions outlined
in Regulation 18704'2(a).(2)-(6), it appears that your economic interest in you. home/ranch is
only indirecrly involved in the governmental deiision.
Mining Claim
From the facts you have provided, your economic interest in the mining claim abuts the
b-oundaries of the proposed casino. Accordingly, your economic interest in this property is
directly involved in the government decision.
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your
economic interest in real property?
Materiality
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a
govemmental decision on a public official's economic interestsls material. (Regulation
18700(a)') Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the economic interest
and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involveJin the agency,s decision.
Under Regulation 18705.2(a)(l), any financial effect of a governmental decision, even
one penny, on real property directly involved in the governmentaldecision is presumed tobe
material' (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1).) This pt"rurnption may be rebutred ontf uy proof that it is
not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision *ill have any financial effect on the
property or, in other words, not even a single penny's effect on the proi.rty.
For properties indirectly involved in a governmental decision, Regulation 1g705.2(bXl)
provides that the financial effect is presumed iot to be material. Howevei , this presttmption may
be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances regarding the govemmental decision,
its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in whilh thelublil official has an
economic interest that make it reasonably foreselabie the decision will have a material financial


File No. I-08-044a
Page No. 6
effect on the real property in which the official has an interest. Examples of these specific
circumstances include when the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of-use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood."
Foreseeability
, 
once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each
of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is .,reasonably
foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic
interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the
materiality standards will be met as a result of the goul.n-"rtal decision. (Regulation
18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be coniidered "reasonably foreseeable,,,but it
must be more rhan a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1g75) 1 Fppcbps. 19g.)
Ultimately; whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, sltpra,at l9g.)
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (ln re oglesby,
supra, at 7l), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determination that must be
left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official.
Home/Ranch
The governmental decisions at issue are related to an effort by the Ione Band of Miwoks
to take land into trust for the constntction of a proposed casino. From the facts provided, these
decisions will directly affect whether or not th; Io;e Band of Miwoks wil ue aure to build a
casino. Accordingly, these facts indicate specific circumstances that make it reasonably
foreseeable that the govemmental decisioni at issue will have a material financial affect on your
home/ranch. It is foreseeable that aproject the magnitude of a casino located approximately one-
half mile from your home/ranch will affect the chaiacter of the neighborhood including, but not
limited to, substantial effects on the "view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions,
or similar traits of the neighbor," as well as lights, crime levels, and property values within the
vicinity of the casino. (Regulation 18705.2br(lXC); see also FraseiAivice Letter, No. A-02-
0s4.)


File No. I-08-044a
Page No. 7
Mining Claim
Based upon the facts provided, you have indicated a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on your economic interest in the mining claim. while ultimately the deiermination of
whether the financial effect is material must b1 left up to your informei judgment, the financial
effect on your economic interest in the mining claimls pi"sumed to be matenal because the
property is within 500 feet of the property subject to th; govemmental decision.
Even if you can rebut the presumption of any financial effect on your economic interest
in the mining claim abutting the site of the casino pioiect and can show no other reasonably
foreseeable financial effects on any other economii interests you may have, the specific
circumstances of the governmental decisions at issue rebut thl presumption or a non-material
effect on your econo_mic interest in your home/ranch and indicaie u ,easorrabiy ror"r".uut"
material financial effect on this interest. -Accordingly, you may not participate in the
governmental decisions related to the efforts by the IonL Band of Miwoks io take land into trust
for the construction of a proposed casino.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception to the
confl ict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's econtmic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect trr" puuri" g"nerally. (section
87103; Regulation 18707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to take part
in a d-ecision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the
"legally required participation" exception. This exception aiplies oniy in certain very specific
circumstances where the govemm"ri agen"y would be paralyzed from acting. (Section g7101;
Regulation 18708.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to your
situation, so we will not address them further.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Commission Counsel
BGL:jgl

or similar traits of the neighbor," as well as lights, crime levels, and property values within the
vicinity of the casino. (Regulation 18705.2br(lXC); see also FraseiAivice Letter, No. A-02-
0s4.)


a
Mining Claim
Based upon the facts provided, you have indicated a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on your economic interest in the mining claim. while ultimately the deiermination of
whether the financial effect is material must b1 left up to your informei judgment, the financial
effect on your economic interest in the mining claimls pi"sumed to be matenal because the
property is within 500 feet of the property subject to th; govemmental decision.
Even if you can rebut the presumption of any financial effect on your economic interest
in the mining claim abutting the site of the casino pioiect and can show no other reasonably
foreseeable financial effects on any other economii interests you may have, the specific
circumstances of the governmental decisions at issue rebut thl presumption or a non-material
effect on your econo_mic interest in your home/ranch and indicaie u ,easorrabiy ror"r".uut"
material financial effect on this interest. -Accordingly, you may not participate in the
governmental decisions related to the efforts by the IonL Band of Miwoks io take land into trust
for the construction of a proposed casino.
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception to the
confl ict-of-interest rules?
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's econtmic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect trr" puuri" g"nerally. (section
87103; Regulation 18707(a).)
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to take part
in a d-ecision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the
"legally required participation" exception. This exception aiplies oniy in certain very specific
circumstances where the govemm"ri agen"y would be paralyzed from acting. (Section g7101;
Regulation 18708.)
You have not presented any facts indicating that these exceptions are applicable to your
situation, so we will not address them further.
